children.text: From Reggaetón to Urbano Latino
Latinizing Reggaetón

The trajectory of reggaetón’s incorporation into the global music industry follows a familiar pattern shared with other Afro-diasporic genres: forms that emerge as localized articulations of Black expressive culture are gradually recontextualized, sanitized, and redistributed through dominant market logics. In its early stages, reggaetón functioned as a site of Black-diasporic resistance in Puerto Rico, drawing from hip-hop and dancehall to articulate the lived realities of working-class and racialized communities, particularly in the
caseríos

of San Juan.  Its early reception reflects this positioning—widely embraced by youth audiences while simultaneously subjected to censorship campaigns that framed the music as morally corrupting and socially destabilizing. Ironically, such efforts only amplified its underground circulation, consolidating reggaetón as a powerful countercultural force.

By the early 2000s, however, reggaetón’s rapid rise—punctuated by the crossover success of Gasolina by Daddy Yankee—positioned the genre for incorporation into U.S. and global media markets. This moment coincided with the so-called “Latin Boom,” in which artists like Ricky Martin, Enrique Iglesias, and Shakira were already establishing the commercial viability of “Latin” music for Anglophone audiences. Within this framework, reggaetón was rebranded as “Latin" hip-hop, a move that expanded its reach while initiating a process of discursive flattening that erased the Black diasporic and transnational origins of the genre. This process of Latinization was not neutral. It operated through what can be understood as a set of racialized and aesthetic “safety frames” that rendered reggaetón legible and profitable to global audiences. These frames privileged artists who conformed more closely to a dominant “Latin look” (light complexion, European features, racially ambiguous presentation) while downplaying or erasing the genre’s Afro-Caribbean origins. Simultaneously, the raciolinguistic enregisterment of Spanish as the defining marker of Latinidad allowed the genre’s linguistic surface to stand in for cultural authenticity, even as the Blackness that originally animated its sonic and performative practices was increasingly backgrounded. In this sense, reggaetón’s crossover depended not only on translation across markets, but on a partial detachment from the Blackness that had initially rendered it politically and culturally potent.
[Solis Miranda's old vs new reggaetón artists picture]

As reggaetón became a dominant force in global popular music, these dynamics intensified. The genre’s sonic templates for embodied performance rooted in Black-diasporic traditions remained central to its appeal, yet their circulation was increasingly mediated by artists and industry structures positioned closer to whiteness, economic privilege, and transnational mobility. The result is a political economy in which Afro-, Afro-Caribbean, and Caribbean expressive forms more broadly are widely consumed as markers of a generalized, non-threatening
urban

Latinidad, while the communities and identities most directly tied to their emergence are rendered less visible within mainstream representations. Reggaetón’s Latinization, then, is not simply a story of expansion, but of transformation: a reconfiguration of the genre’s indexical field in which Blackness, language, and marketability are continuously negotiated.
Below are some of the most pivotal moments of reggaetón's continued transformation and globalization over the past two decades.
Danza Kuduro and Zumba

“Danza Kuduro” marks one of reggaetón’s clearest early-2010s crossings into global dance-pop. Released by Don Omar and Lucenzo in 2010, the song fused reggaetón’s Puerto Rican urbano framework with kuduro, an Angolan-derived dance music that had already circulated through Lusophone African and Portuguese-speaking diasporic networks. Its success reframed reggaetón not only as club music or urban youth expression, but as a participatory global dance template: something to be learned, repeated, choreographed, and embodied across linguistic and national boundaries.

This was amplified by the rise of Zumba, which moved Latin urban rhythms into gyms, dance studios, cruise ships, and community centers. Through Zumba, reggaetón’s dembow-derived rhythmic sensibility became newly legible as “Latin dance” for global audiences, often detached from the genre’s underground history and racial politics. In this moment, reggaetón entered a new circuit of circulation: not only radio and clubs, but choreographed wellness economies where Afro-diasporic rhythmic templates were repackaged as accessible, high-energy, multicultural fun.
Bailando

“Bailando” by Enrique Iglesias featuring Descemer Bueno and Gente de Zona represents a key crossover moment in which reggaetón-adjacent rhythmic and vocal aesthetics were absorbed into a polished Latin pop framework. The track draws from an expanding urbano-pop soundscape in which dembow, Caribbean percussion, flamenco guitar, and Cuban dance music coexist, helping normalize the presence of reggaetón’s rhythmic vocabulary within mainstream Spanish-language pop.

Its global success demonstrated that reggaetón no longer needed to be framed as underground, illicit, or oppositional to circulate widely. Instead, “Bailando” presented a smoother, more romanticized and family-friendly iteration of the genre’s sonic and visual language, foregrounding dance, intimacy, and pan-Latin appeal. In doing so, it helped prepare the conditions for the late-2010s urbano boom: reggaetón could now function as a central infrastructure for Latin pop while still carrying the rhythmic traces of Afro-Caribbean dance forms.
Medellín in the Mix

By the mid-2010s, Medellín emerged as a major center of reggaetón production, shifting the genre’s geography beyond Puerto Rico, Panama, and New York. Artists such as J Balvin, Maluma, and Karol G helped consolidate a Colombian urbano sound that was smoother, more melodic, and more explicitly pop-facing than earlier underground iterations. Their rise redistributed the genre’s authority, positioning Medellín as a site where reggaetón could be refined, commercialized, and exported globally.

J Balvin’s work framed reggaetón as cosmopolitan and fashion-forward, Maluma pushed the genre further into romantic pop and celebrity culture, and Karol G expanded the visibility and sonic range of women within the urbano market. Together, they demonstrate how reggaetón’s templates could be revoiced through Colombian aesthetics and industry infrastructures. Medellín’s emergence thus marks a key moment in the genre’s transnationalization, where reggaetón becomes less tied to a single national origin and more recognizable as a flexible, global pop language.
Latin Trap

By the mid-2010s, Latin trap emerged as a countercurrent to the increasingly polished, Latinized reggaetón circulating through global pop markets. Rooted in the sonic aesthetics of U.S. Southern trap—slower tempos, sparse percussion, heavy bass, minor-key atmospheres, and a renewed emphasis on rap flow—Latin trap allowed Puerto Rican artists to reactivate the underground sensibilities that reggaetón had partially shed during its crossover. Artists such as Anuel AA, Bryant Myers, Ozuna, and later Bad Bunny used trap to foreground street-coded authenticity, emotional excess, and linguistic specificity, often performing in a Puerto Rican Spanish that resisted the flattening pressures of mainstream Latin pop.

In this sense, Latin trap was not a departure from reggaetón’s history so much as a return to one of its foundational impulses: the adaptation of Black U.S. and Afro-Caribbean musical forms to articulate local experiences of marginality, masculinity, pleasure, violence, and survival. Its rise helped re-center Puerto Rico within the urbano landscape at a moment when Colombian pop-reggaetón was becoming dominant, while also expanding what urbano could sound and feel like. Latin trap restored darkness, rawness, and lyrical confrontation to the market, preparing the conditions for artists like Bad Bunny to move fluidly between trap, reggaetón, and broader Caribbean experimentation.
Despacito

The release of “Despacito” by Luis Fonsi featuring Daddy Yankee in 2017 marks a decisive inflection point in reggaetón’s global consolidation. The song demonstrated that a Spanish-language track grounded in reggaetón’s rhythmic sensibilities could dominate global pop charts without translation. Its viral spread, further amplified by the remix featuring Justin Bieber, shattered industry assumptions about the limits of non-English music.

Crucially, “Despacito” presented a polished, melodic, and romanticized iteration of reggaetón, foregrounding acoustic guitar, pop vocal delivery, and sensual—but widely palatable—imagery. This reframed dembow-based production as compatible with global pop intimacy rather than exclusively tied to perreo or underground culture. As a result, reggaetón expanded demographically, becoming central to mainstream global music consumption.

At the same time, its success intensified tensions around language, race, and representation, raising questions about which sounds and identities are most easily globalized. “Despacito” did not simply expand the market—it restructured it, establishing reggaetón as a dominant, yet internally contested, language of global pop.
¿Dónde está Mi Gente?

“Mi Gente” by J Balvin and Willy William represents a parallel trajectory to “Despacito,” foregrounding rhythm and collective participation over melodic crossover. Blending reggaetón with electronic and global club production, the track operates through repetition, chant, and embodied engagement rather than narrative or lyrical complexity.

Its minimal, chant-like structure made it highly scalable across linguistic boundaries, circulating through dance challenges, DJ culture, and social media virality. The remix featuring Beyoncé further amplified its reach while preserving its Spanish-language core, signaling a shift in which global pop artists increasingly adapt to reggaetón’s sonic and linguistic frameworks.

“Mi Gente” demonstrates that reggaetón’s global success does not depend solely on pop softening or romantic framing. Instead, it affirms the power of rhythmic intensity, repetition, and collective address, positioning reggaetón as a shared, transnational dance language grounded in Afro-diasporic musical traditions.
Superbowl LIV

The Super Bowl LIV Halftime Show headlined by Shakira and Jennifer Lopez stands as a landmark moment for reggaetón and Latin music’s visibility within the U.S. mainstream, even though the performance itself was not centered on reggaetón as a genre. Framed primarily through pop, dance, and Latin crossover hits, the show nevertheless strategically incorporated urbano through guest appearances by Bad Bunny and J Balvin, as well as performances of tracks like “Mi Gente” and “I Like It,” alongside reggaetón-inflected moments in Shakira’s own catalog. In doing so, the halftime show positioned reggaetón not as a marginal or niche genre, but as an essential component of contemporary Latin popular music, worthy of inclusion on one of the most visible entertainment stages in the world. This moment signals a broader shift in the cultural economy: reggaetón’s sounds, artists, and aesthetics had become sufficiently normalized—and profitable—to be showcased within a highly curated, national spectacle, even if still embedded within a broader, more palatable Latin pop framework.
Bichota & The Performance of Caribbeanness

“Bichota” by Karol G marks a pivotal moment in the consolidation of reggaetón’s global economy because it makes explicit what earlier crossover successes like “Despacito” and “Mi Gente” had already begun to demonstrate: Caribbeanness is not a liability in global markets—it is profitable. While Karol G had previously operated within the smoother, pop-oriented Medellín urbano aesthetic, “Bichota” represents a deliberate stylistic and linguistic shift toward a more overtly Caribbeanized reggaetón performance. Most notably, this includes her adoption of phonological features associated with Puerto Rican Spanish—features that carry strong indexical ties to reggaetón’s historical centers of production and its Afro-Caribbean roots.

This shift is not incidental. In the wake of “Despacito,” which proved that Spanish-language reggaetón could dominate global charts, and “Mi Gente,” which demonstrated the scalability of rhythm and collective address without linguistic accommodation, “Bichota” moves one step further: it foregrounds the value of sounding Caribbean. The track’s performance does not dilute or neutralize these features for broader accessibility; instead, it amplifies them as stylistic capital. In doing so, Karol G aligns herself more directly with the genre’s perceived locus of authenticity, signaling that proximity to Caribbean linguistic and sonic norms enhances—not limits—global appeal.

At the same time, this moment reveals the continued asymmetries within reggaetón’s political economy. The ability to strategically adopt Caribbean features as a resource underscores how Caribbeanness circulates as a commodifiable aesthetic, one that can be mobilized by artists positioned within different national and racial formations. “Bichota” thus encapsulates a key transformation in the genre’s indexical field: where earlier industry logics often required a distancing from Blackness and Afro-Caribbean specificity to achieve crossover success, the late 2010s demonstrate that these very features can be revalued as desirable markers of authenticity, edge, and marketability.
Bad Bunny

Bad Bunny’s emergence in the late 2010s marks a decisive re-centering of Puerto Rico within reggaetón’s global expansion, not by rejecting commercialization, but by reshaping its terms. His 2020 album “YHLQMDLG” operates as both homage and intervention: sonically, it draws heavily from early-2000s reggaetón and perreo aesthetics, reactivating dembow-driven minimalism, DJ-centric sequencing, and underground textures; semiotically, it reasserts Puerto Rican Spanish—its phonology, cadence, and lexical play—as a primary stylistic resource rather than something to be flattened for crossover. In doing so, the album reframes nostalgia not as regression, but as a deliberate recuperation of reggaetón’s Afro-Caribbean indexical core within a globalized market.

This intervention becomes especially visible in “Yo perreo sola,” a track that simultaneously returns to the sonic template of classic perreo and radically reconfigures its gender politics. By performing in drag and centering the autonomy of women within the dance space, Bad Bunny disrupts reggaetón’s historically masculinist performance norms while preserving the embodied, rhythmic intensity that defines the genre. The result is a layered indexical move: perreo remains intact as a site of Afro-diasporic dance practice, but its social meanings are expanded to include feminist critique, queer visibility, and self-directed pleasure.

With “Un Verano Sin Ti” (2022), Bad Bunny extends this project by scaling Caribbeanness itself as a global organizing aesthetic. The album moves fluidly across reggaetón, dembow, mambo, reggae, house, and indie pop, but remains anchored in a distinctly Caribbean sensibility—sonically, linguistically, and affectively. Rather than presenting reggaetón as a single genre, it positions the Caribbean as a dynamic, internally diverse soundscape whose musical forms can circulate globally without losing their regional grounding. This is not crossover in the earlier sense of adaptation to Anglo pop norms; it is a re-centering of Caribbean expressive culture as the norm to which global audiences attune.

Recent work such as “DTMF” continues this trajectory, further consolidating the idea that Puerto Rican urbano can function simultaneously as local expression and global infrastructure. Across these projects, Bad Bunny demonstrates that reggaetón’s commercial dominance need not rely on the erasure of its Afro-Caribbean roots. Instead, his work shows that linguistic specificity, rhythmic intensity, and Caribbean cultural references can themselves drive mass appeal. In this sense, Bad Bunny does not simply participate in reggaetón’s globalization—he redefines its terms, establishing Caribbeanness, rather than its dilution, as the genre’s most powerful and profitable organizing principle.
Benito Bowl & Beyond
The
Benito Bowl

—the Super Bowl LX Halftime Show headlined by Bad Bunny—marks a historic rupture in the cultural and linguistic politics of global popular music. On February 8, 2026, Bad Bunny became the first artist to headline the Super Bowl halftime show with a performance delivered primarily—and effectively entirely—in Spanish . This was not a moment of translation or accommodation; it was a full-scale reorientation of the stage itself. The most-watched entertainment platform in the United States was reorganized around Puerto Rican Spanish, Caribbean sound, and Afro-diasporic musical forms, with no concessions made to Anglophone expectations.
What makes the “Benito Bowl” especially significant is that it completes a trajectory you’ve been mapping: from
Despacito
proving Spanish-language viability, to
Mi Gente

proving rhythmic scalability, to Bad Bunny demonstrating that neither language nor Caribbeanness needs to be softened at all. The performance—framed as a celebration of Puerto Rican culture, complete with traditional instruments, local iconography, and a reggaetón-heavy setlist—positioned Caribbean expressive culture not as an exotic supplement to American spectacle, but as its organizing center . Even the audience logic shifts here: rather than asking global viewers to understand Spanish, Bad Bunny explicitly reframed participation around feeling and movement (“people only have to worry about dance”) .

In this sense, the “Benito Bowl” is not just representation—it is infrastructural power. It demonstrates that reggaetón and Puerto Rican urbano have reached a point where they can dictate the terms of global visibility, rather than adapt to them. The halftime show becomes less a site where Latin music is included and more a site where U.S. mainstream culture temporarily reorganizes itself around Caribbean norms. What was once underground, racialized, and censored now commands the most surveilled stage in American media, not by erasing its linguistic or cultural specificity, but by amplifying it.
